sections.heading: The challenge of complex spoken discourse
sections.body: At advanced level, listening difficulty is rarely about individual words — it's about tracking the macro-structure: what is the speaker's main point, how are they building their argument, where are the pivots, what is implied vs. stated?

sections.heading: Signpost language
sections.body: Speakers signal their discourse structure explicitly. 'On the one hand... on the other' = contrast coming. 'What I'm really trying to say is...' = simplification/summary. 'Having said that...' = qualification. 'Let me step back...' = perspective shift. These are the structural 'headers' of spoken argument.

sections.heading: Active listening strategies
sections.body: Top-down processing: use your knowledge of the topic to predict what's coming. Bottom-up: focus on key stressed words and discourse markers. Selective attention: don't try to catch every word — identify the main point and the structure.

examples.english: 'What I'm really trying to say is...'
examples.note: Signal: rephrasing/clarification of the core point

exercises.instruction: What discourse function does each phrase signal?
items: 'This brings me to my main argument...'

quiz.question: When a speaker says 'The bottom line is...', what are they signaling?
options: The most important conclusion or key point
A transition to a new topic
quiz.explanation: 'The bottom line is' signals the speaker is about to state the most essential point — their core conclusion.
